text: The surgeon begins laparoscopic cholecystectomy using four ports, performing careful dissection for adhesiolysis. The gallbladder wall appears necrotic with pus oozing out. The liver abscess is drained through the posterior wall of the gallbladder, releasing approximately 100 ml of pus. The gallbladder is dissected free from the colon and omentum.

The surgeon uses a suction cannula for dissection, effectively separating and lifting the gallbladder. Hydro dissection aids in freeing the gallbladder, allowing examination of Calot's triangle. The omentum is dissected away from the gallbladder neck, avoiding the duodenum and other vital structures. Both posterior and anterior dissections are performed to identify the cystic duct and cystic artery, which are clearly visualized.

The surgeon performs further dissection, opening the ampulla of the gallbladder to remove stones, which are visible within the ampulla. The stones are extracted and placed into a plastic bag. The distal gallbladder is separated further from the neck for proper retraction. The thick, inflamed, and gangrenous gallbladder wall is partially excised to visualize the liver abscess cavity and the necrotic mucosa of the gallbladder's posterior wall.

The gallbladder wall is placed inside a plastic bag. The surgeon continues dissection, confirming the cystic duct and cystic arteries. The neck of the gallbladder is separated, and the cystic artery is clipped and cut with scissors to ensure no bile leak. The cystic duct is dissected free, doubly clipped, and divided. A tributary of the cystic artery is clipped and divided, and the remnant of the neck of the gallbladder is dissected away from the liver.

The surgeon carefully safeguards vital structures while leaving behind a portion of the gallbladder wall that is adherent to the liver. The mucosa of this adherent section is cauterized. The remaining gallbladder is separated from the liver bed, revealing that the majority of the posterior wall is necrosed and nearly absent.

The gallbladder is completely separated and placed into a specimen bag. The surgeon inspects the liver abscess cavity, performing thorough lavage and washout. A large-bore drain is inserted into the liver cavity to facilitate drainage.
